figures.caption: Fig. 2 Directional ASI family. a Directional tile template. The tile template is composed of three magnet orientations {θA, θB , θC}, arranged along the diagonal. b Space of directional geometries. All combinations of magnet orientations are considered, plotted according to their directionality |D| and ferromagnetism F . Each point in the plot corresponds to a 12 × 12 geometry and a three clock fields. The number of possible clock fields per tile depends on the magnet orientations (see Methods). The Pareto front of the search space is highlighted in orange, with six example tiles highlighted as Ti according to the order they appear along the Pareto front. c Tiles and their corresponding timeevolution. Six example tiles from the Pareto front are shown. The top row depicts the tile, while the bottom row shows time-evolution maps of the corresponding geometry. An initial center domain is gradually grown over time using astroid clocking. The shade of gray indicates the time step of growth, from black at initialization time to light gray towards the end, with white indicating no activity.

Fig. 5 Experimental directional domain growth of T18. a Average intensity of 31 XMCDPEEM images of 10 ABC clock cycles (30 field pulses), starting from the polarized (white) state. The intensity of the magnets indicate when the magnets switch in the series, where the dark magnets switch first and the light magnets switch last. The imaged area is 19µm × 19µm. b-g Snapshots of the blue and orange regions of the array, at three intervals in the clock series: after b-c three, d-e six and f-g nine clock cycles. The centers of mass of the tracked domains are marked by colored dots in all snapshots, and its movement from the previous snapshot is shown by the white arrows.

Fig. 7 Phase space of directional growth. a Schematic of the 3D phase space of directional growth in the T18 directional ASI, where hue represents direction and lightness represents the speed of growth (light is slow/no growth and dark is fast/avalanching growth). The three slices shown in b-d are marked in gray in a. 1-8 show examples of the variation of growth we find using varying field strengths, where the growth starts from the black initial domain and follows the gradient. The colored arrow marks the direction of movement of the center of mass. 1 and 8 are the northwest and southeast growth regimes, respectively.

Fig. 1: Heatmap of the influence asymmetry |Iji| − |Iij | between two spins i and j, as a function of their orientation θi and θj . For each θi, θj pair, we find the external field where the influence asymmetry is maximized. In other words, the heatmap shows where the influence from i → j is greater than j → i. Dark areas in the heatmap correspond to spin orientations that do not allow for asymmetry i → j. We see immediately that there can be no asymmetry when the spins have the same orientation θi = θj . This is due to the mirror symmetry of the dipolar fields, where hij dip = hji dip. The diagonal symmetry seen in the heatmap stem from the mirror symmetry of the astroid around the horizontal axis, obtained by the transformation (θi, θj) → (−θi,−θj).

Fig. 4: A full series of XMCD-PEEM images showing domain growth in the southeast growth regime. Growth starts from the polarized state (white) and domains appear black. Images show the full 100 × 100 directional ASI based on tile T18 consisting of 220 nm×80 nm×10 nm stadium-shaped nanomagnets. Note that the three outermost layers of magnets are slimmer (220 nm× 70 nm× 10 nm) and thus never switch. The ASI is imaged after every field pulse for 10 full ABC cycles.

Fig. 6: Reservoir computing with 100× 100 T18. a Time evolution of T18 subject to a binary input time series. Input bits are encoded along the left edge, as indicated by the arrows into the shaded regions. An astroid clocking protocol is applied between each input to move information southeast (ABCabcabc × 3). Denoted below each snapshot are the input bits which have been encoded so far. b Reservoir computing performance on two benchmark tasks: memory capacity (MC ) and delayed n-bit parity (PARITYn).

Fig. 3 The T18 geometry. a A close-up of a T18 ASI. The three sublattices, LA, LB and LC are marked with solid, dashed and dotted outlines, respectively. In the top half, the colors correspond to the state of the magnets, as indicated by the white arrows. In the bottom half, the per spin influence vector Ii is shown for each magnet. The total directionality vector D is shown in the inset. b The six clock fields used to grow (A, B and C) and shrink (a, b and c) domains. The switching astroids of the three sublattices are also shown. c-e Influence maps for the reference magnet (gray) belonging to each of the three sublattices, subject to their respective clock field. The blue magnets have a stabilizing effect on the reference, while red magnets have a destabilizing effect. The influence maps correspond to the polarized state.

Fig. 8 Reservoir computing with T18. a Time evolution of T18 subject to a binary input time series. Input bits are encoded along the left edge, as indicated by the arrows into the shaded regions. An astroid clocking protocol is applied between each input to move information southeast (ABCabcabc× 3). Denoted below each snapshot are the input bits which have been encoded so far. b Reservoir computing performance on two benchmark tasks: memory capacity (MC ) and delayed n-bit parity (PARITYn ).
